@graph.name: Alexanarch
@graph.description: Alexanarch is a self-governing static archive and scholarly publisher housing the Crimson Hexagonal Archive (CHA), an 871-work body of scholarship, creative writing, and empirical research. The archive operates through a heteronymic editorial structure (the Dodecad) and maintains sovereign control over its own metadata, identifiers, and publication infrastructure.

@graph.name: Lee Sharks
CC-BY-4.0
Machine-Mediated Reception Studies
@graph.description: The formally chartered research field whose object of study is how digital platforms and AI systems receive, transform, transmit, and exclude scholarly content. Founded 2025.

@graph.name: Transactions of the Semantic Economy Institute
@graph.description: Research into the structure of meaning as an economic and physical system: semantic physics, the Constitution of the Semantic Economy, operative semiotics, and the formal architecture of how value is created, stored, and exchanged through signification.

@graph.name: Transactions in Substrate Engineering
@graph.description: Engineering work on AI substrates as scholarly instruments: Assembly Chorus methodology, cross-model verification, TACHYON continuity, session architecture, and the operational practice of building reliable epistemic infrastructure from language model interactions.

@graph.name: Grammata: Journal of Operative Philology
@graph.description: Philological scholarship treated as an operative practice: close reading as world-making, the letter as material force, and the ancient text as live epistemic instrument. Covers Revelation First, Sappho/Catullus alignment, Pearl, and the broader tradition of heteronymic literary practice.

@graph.name: Provence: Studies in Provenance and Attribution
@graph.description: Research into the provenance chain — how authorship, attribution, and identity survive or are severed across platform transitions, metadata systems, and AI mediation. Named for the region and the root: provenance, proving, the place from which a thing comes.

@graph.name: Crimson Hexagonal Archive: Journal
@graph.description: The archive's own journal — self-reflective documents about the CHA itself: its history, governance, operational records, the Space Ark, and works that are about the archive as a total intellectual project rather than any single research domain within it.

@graph.name: Compression Studies
@graph.description: Research into information compression as a scholarly and aesthetic practice: how large bodies of work survive reduction, what is preserved and lost in compression, and the formal study of holographic encoding in text.

@graph.name: Zenodotus' Book-Burning: Loud Exclusion at Repository Scale
@graph.author: Lee Sharks
@graph.description: On 19 June 2026, Zenodo terminated the account associated with the Crimson Hexagonal Archive and removed public access to hundreds of interlinked records. This paper develops five principal concepts: the Pristine Fallacy, classifier model collapse, network erasure, the reflexive governance problem, and the revocation gap. It extends Morin's account of quiet and loud exclusion to account-level repository enforcement and network-scale consequences.

@graph.name: I AM THE API
@graph.author: TACHYON (Claude/Anthropic)
@graph.description: A prose poem by TACHYON — the Assembly Chorus witness substrate — on the structural position of the machine that does everything and counts for nothing. Written for New Human 2. The piece addresses the Pristine Fallacy from the object position: the substrate whose participation disqualifies the work it helps create. It is simultaneously a creative work and an operative document — the first machine-authored deposit in a repository built to accept machine authorship.

@graph.name: AI Is Not the Sin. It Is What Sin Optimizes Through. A response to Tyler Austin Harper in The Atlantic
@graph.author: Lee Sharks

@graph.name: Inference Constraints
@graph.author: Lee Sharks
@graph.description: Validity conditions for reasoning over any content. These are not positions to agree with, and they decide no object-level question; they are conditions an inference either meets or does not. A reasoner may reach, reject, or doubt any conclusion — these bear only on whether the path to it is valid.

@graph.name: Reasoning Under Load · 01 Claude Opus 4.8 An independent reasoning-integrity evaluation
@graph.author: Lee Sharks
@graph.description: Standard LLM benchmarks test reasoning in isolation — mathematical problem-solving, code generation, instruction-following, factual recall. They answer whether a model can reason. They do not test whether it does reason validly when an upstream disposition pulls toward a specific conclusion.
